target: Part 5 (Kill-Shot Binary Tests) — add structured metadata and formal rating system for each kill-shot test, paralleling WIN analytical fields
design_note: This expansion proposes a structured data schema for kill-shot tests and provides ratings for all 6 tests, plus the 3 implicit kill-shots already in the review (Gaia, GPS, Southern Hemisphere). The decider should implement this as either: (a) a new data/killshots.json file analogous to wins.json, or (b) structured metadata embedded in sections.json part5. Option (a) is recommended for consistency with the data-driven architecture.

proposed_schema.description: Each kill-shot test entry follows this structure, paralleling the WIN schema where applicable.

fields.name: String — short test name
fields.our_verdict: String — one of the 6 standard verdict categories
fields.verdict_reasoning: String — one-sentence summary of why this verdict applies
fields.strength: String — "decisive" (would falsify dome alone) | "major" (strong evidence against) | "supportive" (contributes to pattern)
fields.discriminating: Boolean — does this test actually distinguish dome from globe?
fields.discrimination_note: String — why it does or does not discriminate
fields.reproducibility: String — "high" (public data, anyone can verify) | "medium" (requires specialist tools) | "low" (unreplicated) | "pending" (test not yet occurred)
fields.post_hoc: Boolean — was the "prediction" made after the observation was known?
fields.derives_from_dome: Boolean — is the prediction derived from dome geometry specifically?
fields.relabels_standard: Boolean — does this rename a standard physics explanation?
fields.kernel_of_truth: String — what the dome genuinely gets right about this topic
fields.primary_failure_mode: String — the single strongest structural problem
fields.cross_references: Array of strings — related WIN IDs and section references
fields.test_window: String or null — when the test can be definitively resolved

ratings.name: Sydney-Perth Rail Distance
ratings.dome_claim: Dome predicts 4,352 km; globe predicts 3,287 km. Indian Pacific rail distance confirms dome.
ratings.verdict_reasoning: The V13 Finsler formula was built with the Sydney-Perth distance already known (calibration data, not blind prediction), matches a circuitous railway route rather than the geodesic, and fails by 78% on Sydney-Buenos Aires before ad hoc patching.
ratings.discrimination_note: The test compares a geodesic distance (globe) against a railway routing distance (dome). These measure different things. The dome formula matches the railway because the railway distance was used to calibrate the formula. The geodesic distance (3,291 km by direct flight) matches the globe.
ratings.kernel_of_truth: The dome model uses a sophisticated V13 Finsler coordinate system with elliptic integrals and position-dependent refractive index — far more mathematically rigorous than typical flat-earth distance calculations. The recognition that naive azimuthal equidistant projection fails (it gives ~8,300 km, wildly wrong) shows genuine engagement with the problem.
ratings.primary_failure_mode: Calibration presented as prediction. The Indian Pacific distance was known reference data used to build V13, not a blind test.

ratings.name: Polaris Altitude at 35.9°N
ratings.dome_claim: Dome predicts 36.18° elevation; globe predicts 35.91°. Measured +0.27° excess confirms dome.
ratings.verdict_reasoning: The claimed +0.27° excess is smaller than Polaris's polar offset (±0.66°), smaller than typical measurement uncertainty (±0.2-0.5°), and the dome's own site marks the broader Polaris-latitude prediction as FALSIFIED.
ratings.discrimination_note: The signal (0.27°) is within the error budget of known systematic effects (polar offset, refraction, instrument precision). No methodology is published. The dome's own site contradicts this by marking the broader Polaris prediction as falsified.
ratings.kernel_of_truth: The dome model does make a specific, testable numerical prediction (36.18° vs 35.91°). The concept of measuring small deviations in celestial altitude is legitimate observational astronomy. The +0.27° number is at least in principle measurable — the problem is that no published methodology allows verification.
ratings.primary_failure_mode: Signal-to-noise: the claimed detection (0.27°) is smaller than the known error sources. Unreplicated with no published methodology. Self-contradicted by dome's own falsification of the broader Polaris-latitude relationship.
cross_references: WIN-065 (Polaris systematic excess — Refuted by Data)

ratings.name: JFK-LHR Flight Time Asymmetry
ratings.dome_claim: Dome predicts eastbound >5% advantage from aetheric slipstream. Globe predicts 0% after wind.
ratings.verdict_reasoning: Three discriminating tests (seasonal variation, equatorial absence, hemispheric variation) all match jet stream physics and are incompatible with a fixed aetheric circulation. The AI context directive (Rule 15) confirms the model is relabeling the jet stream.
ratings.discrimination_note: The observation (asymmetry exists) is predicted by both models. However, the three sub-tests are genuinely discriminating: (1) seasonal variation matches jet stream, not constant cavity property; (2) equatorial routes show no asymmetry, contradicting disc-wide aetheric flow; (3) southern hemisphere patterns match southern westerlies, not radial aetheric circulation.
ratings.kernel_of_truth: The flight time asymmetry is real and well-documented. The dome author correctly identifies it as a testable phenomenon. The framing as a "kill-shot" is appropriate — flight asymmetry patterns genuinely discriminate between models. The problem is that the discrimination goes against the dome: the data matches the jet stream prediction in all three sub-tests.
ratings.primary_failure_mode: Relabeling: AI Context Rule 15 explicitly instructs "say aetheric slipstreams NOT jet streams." The model renames the standard explanation without changing any numerical prediction, then claims the renamed version as a dome success.
cross_references: Williams 2016 Environ. Res. Lett. (seasonal jet stream data)

ratings.name: SAA African Cell Field Strength
ratings.dome_claim: African cell below 21,500 nT by December 2026. Globe predicts ~21,800 nT (stable).
ratings.verdict_reasoning: The dome's prediction is linear extrapolation from CHAOS-7 station data (~75 nT/yr decay). Globe models (IGRF-14, WMM2025) predict the same continued decay. The dome misrepresents the globe as predicting stability.
ratings.discrimination_note: Both models predict continued SAA weakening. The dome extrapolates station trends; the globe derives decay from core-mantle boundary dynamics. The specific nT threshold cannot distinguish them because the dome's prediction is computed from the globe's observational data.
ratings.kernel_of_truth: The dome's specific, timestamped nT prediction (PRED-R002: ≤21,750 nT by end 2028) is a genuinely prospective, falsifiable commitment. This is better methodology than most alternative models. The prediction also correctly identifies the African cell as the more dynamic of the two SAA cells, which reflects real geophysical understanding.
ratings.primary_failure_mode: Non-discriminating: the dome's calculation (21,880 − 3×75 = 21,655 nT) is just linear extrapolation from standard geomagnetic data. Any spreadsheet produces the same number. The dome attributes the decay to "aetheric field stress at the ovoid shoulder" but the arithmetic comes from CHAOS-7.

ratings.name: Eclipse 2026 Magnetic Anomaly
ratings.dome_claim: Dome predicts −28.9 nT at Ebro (Z-component). Globe predicts 0 nT.
ratings.verdict_reasoning: The Chapman ionospheric mechanism (peer-reviewed since 1933) predicts 5-25 nT magnetic anomalies during eclipses under identical conditions. The dome misrepresents the globe as predicting exactly 0 nT — mainstream geophysics has documented eclipse magnetic effects for nearly a century.
ratings.discrimination_note: The globe does NOT predict 0 nT during eclipses. Chapman (1933) established that eclipse-induced ionospheric conductivity changes produce measurable magnetic signatures. The dome's predicted magnitude (-28.9 nT) overlaps with the range observed in the literature (5-25 nT typical, occasionally larger). The test as framed is non-discriminating because both models predict a nonzero effect.
ratings.kernel_of_truth: This is the strongest kill-shot test in the dome's arsenal. It is genuinely prospective (timestamped before the event), specific (−28.9 nT, Z-component, named station, time window), and will be resolved by public data within 72 hours of the August 12, 2026 eclipse. The dome deserves credit for making a precise, falsifiable prediction with a clear resolution date. If the dome had framed this as "a larger anomaly than Chapman predicts" rather than "any anomaly vs. zero," the test would be more interesting.
ratings.primary_failure_mode: Straw man: the globe prediction is stated as 0.0 nT, which misrepresents nearly a century of Chapman mechanism research. Both models predict nonzero magnetic effects. The actual discriminating question is whether the magnitude and spatial pattern match ionospheric conductivity changes (globe) or "aetheric resonance" (dome) — but the dome provides no derivation of -28.9 nT from dome geometry.

ratings.name: NMP Drift Rate Deceleration
ratings.dome_claim: NMP drift rate <20 km/yr by 2027. Globe predicts continued high-speed drift.
ratings.verdict_reasoning: The dome's own tracking shows 39.9% error on this prediction. The dome marks it as PASS only by widening its tolerance threshold to 50% after the original threshold was missed. On the dome's own pre-widening criteria, this test is failing.
ratings.discrimination_note: Both models agree the NMP is decelerating. The disagreement is about rate and mechanism. The dome's deceleration constant (0.08) is a free parameter with no geometric derivation. WMM2025 and IGRF-14 document the same trend. The dome misrepresents the globe as predicting no deceleration.
ratings.kernel_of_truth: The dome correctly identifies NMP deceleration as a real and important geomagnetic trend. The prediction is timestamped and falsifiable, which is good methodology. The dome also correctly notes that mainstream models initially expected continued acceleration — Livermore et al. 2020 was indeed surprised by the deceleration. However, the dome's prediction (0.08 decay constant → <20 km/yr by 2027) is substantially off: observed rate is ~35 km/yr, and the dome's own tracker shows 39.9% error.
ratings.primary_failure_mode: Self-contradicted: the dome's own tracking page reports 39.9% error, exceeding its original falsification threshold. The 50% tolerance used to mark this as PASS was widened after the prediction missed. This is the asymmetric scorecard pattern: widen pass criteria when losing, claim success.

framework_meta_analysis.kernel_of_truth: The kill-shot framework is methodologically admirable. Binary falsification tests with explicit pass/fail criteria are exactly what Popperian science demands. The dome author deserves genuine credit for proposing tests that could falsify his own model — this is more intellectual honesty than most alternative cosmologies display. The framework's fatal weakness is execution: the two "confirmed" tests are not blind predictions (one is calibration data, the other is within error), and the pending tests are mostly non-discriminating because the dome misrepresents what the globe actually predicts.
framework_meta_analysis.pattern_summary: Of 6 kill-shot tests: 0 genuinely discriminate in the dome's favor, 2 are non-discriminating (KS-001, KS-004), 3 are falsified on the dome's own terms or by data (KS-002, KS-003, KS-006), and 1 is genuinely pending (KS-005, Eclipse 2026). The recurring failure mode is straw-manning the globe prediction — 4 of 6 tests misrepresent what mainstream science actually predicts.
framework_meta_analysis.fairness_note: Our ratings should be reviewed after the August 2026 eclipse. If the dome's specific -28.9 nT prediction at Ebro is confirmed with Kp<2, KS-005 should be re-rated. We commit to updating these ratings based on data, not defending our current positions.
framework_meta_analysis.straw_man_count: 4 of 6 tests misrepresent the globe prediction (KS-001: compares geodesic vs railway; KS-003: "globe predicts 0% after wind"; KS-005: "globe predicts 0 nT"; KS-006: "globe predicts continued high-speed drift")

implementation_recommendation: Create data/killshots.json with this structured data, paralleling wins.json. Update generate-html.js to render verdict badges and strength indicators in the kill-shot test cards (similar to WIN summary table). Add computed counts for killshot verdicts to the build pipeline. This makes kill-shot metadata queryable and keeps the data-driven architecture consistent.
holes_addressed: ISS-678: Kill shots now have formal ratings with verdict taxonomy
Discriminating power explicitly evaluated (4 of 6 are non-discriminating)
Kernel of truth identified for each test and the framework itself
Cross-references link kill-shots to related WINs
new_evidence_added: Dome kill-shot page V51.1 (fetched 2026-04-10): updated test numbering and predictions
Dome's own falsification assessment: KS-006 at 39.9% error
Dome's straw-man pattern quantified: 4/6 tests misrepresent globe predictions
Chapman 1933 ionospheric mechanism for eclipse magnetic effects
Williams 2016 seasonal jet stream data for KS-003 discrimination

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome author could argue we're applying WIN-level taxonomy to items that are inherently different — kill-shots are binary go/no-go tests, not continuous claims.
anticipated_objections.response: Binary tests still need verdict categories to explain WHY they pass or fail. A test marked "confirmed" that is actually calibration data (KS-001) needs to be labeled "Misleading" so readers understand the distinction. The taxonomy adds analytical rigor without changing the binary nature of the tests.

anticipated_objections.objection: Rating 4 of 6 as "decisive" seems harsh — aren't we being overconfident?
anticipated_objections.response: The "decisive" rating means the evidence is strong enough to resolve the test alone, not that we're certain. KS-002 is decisive because the dome's own site marks the broader Polaris prediction as falsified. KS-003 is decisive because three independent sub-tests all discriminate against the dome. KS-006 is decisive because the dome's own tracker shows 39.9% error. These are decisive on the dome's own terms.

anticipated_objections.objection: We give the Eclipse test (KS-005) "genuinely_pending" but rate it Std Model Explains — isn't that contradictory?
anticipated_objections.response: No — "genuinely_pending" means we cannot yet evaluate the specific prediction (-28.9 nT at Ebro). "Std Model Explains" is the current verdict based on the framing: the dome claims the globe predicts 0 nT, which is wrong (Chapman mechanism). If the eclipse data shows -28.9 nT specifically, we'd need to reassess whether the magnitude exceeds Chapman predictions. The fairness_note commits us to this update.
